term.gloss: The thesis that everything that exists is physical, or supervenes on the physical.
The thesis that the mental and the physical are fundamentally distinct.
The view that phenomenal consciousness is an illusion — what we report as qualia are functional states that lack phenomenal properties.
An entity's expressed interest counter to the second law of thermodynamics.
The view that mental phenomena are causally inert byproducts of physical processes.
Any explanation for consciousness that departs so far from a conventional understanding of reality as to render an expansion of the term "physicalism" meaningless — e.g., consciousness as the soul.

inline.find: gets at an important implication of consciousness— namely, that we ought to suspect further aspects of reality than we can today observe

replace.claim: gets at an important implication of consciousness— namely, that we ought to suspect further aspects of reality than we can today observe

inline.find: the author assumes that we could not explain consciousness through physicalism

replace.claim: the author assumes that we could not explain consciousness through physicalism

inline.find: the author assumes that consciousness is the basis for morality, or that our moral obligations are primarily due toward conscious beings

replace.claim: the author assumes that consciousness is the basis for morality, or that our moral obligations are primarily due toward conscious beings

inline.find: I suspect this happens because we sneak in non-physicalist assumptions about consciousness into discussions about morality.

replace.claim: I suspect this happens because we sneak in non-physicalist assumptions about consciousness into discussions about morality.

inline.find: So, I would like to defend the author’s claim that consciousness ought to cause us to suppose further unobserved aspects of reality (which could be very different from the reality we know), while also defending a purely physicalist explanation for consciousness, and decoupling consciousness from morality.

replace.claim: So, I would like to defend the author’s claim that consciousness ought to cause us to suppose further unobserved aspects of reality (which could be very different from the reality we know), while also defending a purely physicalist explanation for consciousness, and decoupling consciousness from morality.

inline.find: The distinction is important, as we otherwise backdoor capabilities like judgement and agency into consciousness without sufficient justification.

replace.claim: The distinction is important, as we otherwise backdoor capabilities like judgement and agency into consciousness without sufficient justification.

inline.find: I believe this is the crux of _why_ so many of us intuit consciousness as a prerequisite for morality, when I think it is very possibly not the case.

replace.claim: I believe this is the crux of _why_ so many of us intuit consciousness as a prerequisite for morality, when I think it is very possibly not the case.

inline.find: The author introduces us to his fourth world by supposing three distinct domains to reality: the physical world, the mathematical world, and consciousness.

replace.claim: The author introduces us to his fourth world by supposing three distinct domains to reality: the physical world, the mathematical world, and consciousness.

inline.find: The author later claims that a non-conscious, robot civilization would never suspect consciousness exists.

replace.claim: The author later claims that a non-conscious, robot civilization would never suspect consciousness exists.

inline.find: I surmise the author is pointing at some kind of dualism, with mathematics and logic being a third, metaphysical entity.

replace.claim: I surmise the author is pointing at some kind of dualism, with mathematics and logic being a third, metaphysical entity.

inline.find: The difficulty with dualist claims is that they either rely on a “mystical,” non-physical substance, or they rely on semantic qualifications for the physical world.

replace.claim: The difficulty with dualist claims is that they either rely on a “mystical,” non-physical substance, or they rely on semantic qualifications for the physical world.

inline.find: My point is that nearly all explanations for consciousness ought to collapse towards physicalism, because any fundamental discoveries required to explain consciousness also compel us to redefine physics accordingly.

replace.claim: My point is that nearly all explanations for consciousness ought to collapse towards physicalism, because any fundamental discoveries required to explain consciousness also compel us to redefine physics accordingly.

inline.find: I will retain the term “mysticism” for any explanations that depart so far from a conventional understanding of reality as to render an expansion of the term “physicalism” meaningless, i.e. if consciousness is actually the soul.

replace.claim: I will retain the term “mysticism” for any explanations that depart so far from a conventional understanding of reality as to render an expansion of the term “physicalism” meaningless, i.e. if consciousness is actually the soul.

inline.find: If we accept a physicalist explanation of phenomena as at least _possible_, then we should not assume a robot civilization could never discover consciousness.

replace.claim: If we accept a physicalist explanation of phenomena as at least _possible_, then we should not assume a robot civilization could never discover consciousness.

inline.find: Our experience of existence _is_ phenomenal, so I don’t see a way to refute the hard problem without asserting we are all already zombies (I experience, yet sadly I do not exist).

replace.claim: Our experience of existence _is_ phenomenal, so I don’t see a way to refute the hard problem without asserting we are all already zombies (I experience, yet sadly I do not exist).

inline.find: But illusionism is right to question what and when something is actually phenomenal.

replace.claim: But illusionism is right to question what and when something is actually phenomenal.

inline.find: Because when referring to consciousness, we are often actually referring to the reactive and evaluative machinery _behind_ consciousness, e.g. cognition, and not to phenomena themselves.

replace.claim: Because when referring to consciousness, we are often actually referring to the reactive and evaluative machinery _behind_ consciousness, e.g. cognition, and not to phenomena themselves.

inline.find: I’ll refer to this as the difference between an _extrinsic_ and _intrinsic_ interpretation of consciousness. If we take a physicalist position of the hard problem of consciousness, then we can suppose phenomena are either _intrinsic_ in neurophysiological processes in a way that we do not yet understand, or that phenomena are _extrinsic_ to neurophysiological processes.

replace.claim: I’ll refer to this as the difference between an _extrinsic_ and _intrinsic_ interpretation of consciousness. If we take a physicalist position of the hard problem of consciousness, then we can suppose phenomena are either _intrinsic_ in neurophysiological processes in a way that we do not yet understand, or that phenomena are _extrinsic_ to neurophysiological processes.

inline.find: My reading of the dominant theories of consciousness (e.g., IIT, Global Workspace Theory) is that they lean toward an intrinsic view.

replace.claim: My reading of the dominant theories of consciousness (e.g., IIT, Global Workspace Theory) is that they lean toward an intrinsic view.

inline.find: any extrinsic view will require a mysterious “something else” to explain consciousness, which must then causally interact with our neurophysiology, unless we are willing to accept consciousness as a mere epiphenomenal side-effect.

replace.claim: any extrinsic view will require a mysterious “something else” to explain consciousness, which must then causally interact with our neurophysiology, unless we are willing to accept consciousness as a mere epiphenomenal side-effect.

inline.find: I still find it much more _likely_ that consciousness is somehow intrinsic to neurophysiology.

replace.claim: I still find it much more _likely_ that consciousness is somehow intrinsic to neurophysiology.

inline.find: An intrinsic theory requires less deviation from contemporary neuroscience, as we don’t have to posit neuroscience is somehow insufficient to explain mental processes (no c-particles intervene)

replace.claim: An intrinsic theory requires less deviation from contemporary neuroscience, as we don’t have to posit neuroscience is somehow insufficient to explain mental processes (no c-particles intervene)

inline.find: we can neatly sidestep some of the problems of epiphenomenalism (certain mental states are intrinsically experiential)

replace.claim: we can neatly sidestep some of the problems of epiphenomenalism (certain mental states are intrinsically experiential)

inline.find: So, if we suppose an intrinsic, physicalist theory of consciousness, then our experience of pain is by definition _inseparable_ from the pain reaction itself. It is, in fact, _nonsensical_ to discuss an experience of pain distinct from the neurophysiological activity associated with pain. That is pain; the thing itself _is_ the neurophysiology\!

replace.claim: So, if we suppose an intrinsic, physicalist theory of consciousness, then our experience of pain is by definition _inseparable_ from the pain reaction itself. It is, in fact, _nonsensical_ to discuss an experience of pain distinct from the neurophysiological activity associated with pain. That is pain; the thing itself _is_ the neurophysiology\!

inline.find: I suspect illusionism is right that there is nothing like a c-particle.

replace.claim: I suspect illusionism is right that there is nothing like a c-particle.

inline.find: There is no independent experience or qualia of a good cup of coffee—there is only the experience of our own neurophysiology tasting the coffee and evaluating it as good. Consciousness itself is not responsible for judgment and evaluations—that work is all done by the mechanics of neurophysiology. Consciousness does not “intervene” to make its own decisions.

replace.claim: There is no independent experience or qualia of a good cup of coffee—there is only the experience of our own neurophysiology tasting the coffee and evaluating it as good. Consciousness itself is not responsible for judgment and evaluations—that work is all done by the mechanics of neurophysiology. Consciousness does not “intervene” to make its own decisions.

inline.find: I’d now like to consider the ethics downstream of a physicalist, intrinsic understanding of consciousness. Namely, if consciousness is intrinsic to neurophysiological activity, then why should ethics fix itself on the conscious aspects of that activity? Is this not an arbitrary distinction? Should we not rather respect the _preferences_ those activities represent?

replace.claim: I’d now like to consider the ethics downstream of a physicalist, intrinsic understanding of consciousness. Namely, if consciousness is intrinsic to neurophysiological activity, then why should ethics fix itself on the conscious aspects of that activity? Is this not an arbitrary distinction? Should we not rather respect the _preferences_ those activities represent?

inline.find: I suspect that phenomena, as intrinsic properties of neurophysiology, are undifferentiated.

replace.claim: I suspect that phenomena, as intrinsic properties of neurophysiology, are undifferentiated.

inline.find: no phenomenon seems to me inherently good or bad. Experiencing happiness is distinct from experiencing happiness as good.

replace.claim: no phenomenon seems to me inherently good or bad. Experiencing happiness is distinct from experiencing happiness as good.

inline.find: Perhaps one could object that there is no experience of happiness without wanting happiness to continue, or no experience of suffering without wanting suffering to stop; that the mental state cannot be differentiated from its reaction.

replace.claim: Perhaps one could object that there is no experience of happiness without wanting happiness to continue, or no experience of suffering without wanting suffering to stop; that the mental state cannot be differentiated from its reaction.

inline.find: But is this not just another reason to focus on the reaction, or the expressed preference, rather than the experience itself?

replace.claim: But is this not just another reason to focus on the reaction, or the expressed preference, rather than the experience itself?

inline.find: if you would agree that we have a moral obligation not to cause an alien pain, then I think you should say yes, we are both morally obliged to allow the observed entity to act according to their inferred preference

replace.claim: if you would agree that we have a moral obligation not to cause an alien pain, then I think you should say yes, we are both morally obliged to allow the observed entity to act according to their inferred preference

inline.find: You might be thinking that the real difference is the plant’s response to light is entirely automatic, while we are deeply cognitive agents. You can’t compare our preferences to those of a plant\!

replace.claim: You might be thinking that the real difference is the plant’s response to light is entirely automatic, while we are deeply cognitive agents. You can’t compare our preferences to those of a plant\!

inline.find: But I think this overlooks how dumbly reactive a lot of preferences are, even if they are unclearly expressed.

replace.claim: But I think this overlooks how dumbly reactive a lot of preferences are, even if they are unclearly expressed.

inline.find: We might qualify that a preference should represent an entity’s interests counter to the second law of thermodynamics.

replace.claim: We might qualify that a preference should represent an entity’s interests counter to the second law of thermodynamics.

inline.find: I anticipate several counterarguments to a naïve definition of preference, with potentially absurd conclusions. For example, suppose the alien also noticed your rotating fan. Wouldn’t the alien have to suppose a moral obligation to the fan as well? And how do we avoid moral equivalences between turning off a fan and trimming a plant and killing a person?

replace.claim: I anticipate several counterarguments to a naïve definition of preference, with potentially absurd conclusions. For example, suppose the alien also noticed your rotating fan. Wouldn’t the alien have to suppose a moral obligation to the fan as well? And how do we avoid moral equivalences between turning off a fan and trimming a plant and killing a person?

inline.find: These are legitimate lines of critique that a moral framework using preference as the qualifier for moral obligation would have to answer. But it’s easy to imagine different mechanisms to do this, such as by defining some heuristic for the “strength” of preference, similar to the way utilitarianism thinks about utility, or looking at the reversibility of decisions.

replace.claim: These are legitimate lines of critique that a moral framework using preference as the qualifier for moral obligation would have to answer. But it’s easy to imagine different mechanisms to do this, such as by defining some heuristic for the “strength” of preference, similar to the way utilitarianism thinks about utility, or looking at the reversibility of decisions.

inline.find: I am optimistic that in every instance where one might be tempted to evaluate ethics on the basis of consciousness, one could instead insert preference.

replace.claim: I am optimistic that in every instance where one might be tempted to evaluate ethics on the basis of consciousness, one could instead insert preference.

inline.find: What I question is how one can be confident that consciousness is unique?

replace.claim: What I question is how one can be confident that consciousness is unique?

inline.find: We already know there is at least _one_ aspect to reality that is utterly unobservable to an outsider. We would have no concept of phenomena if it were not for our own minds.

replace.claim: We already know there is at least _one_ aspect to reality that is utterly unobservable to an outsider. We would have no concept of phenomena if it were not for our own minds.

inline.find: Is this not the best possible evidence that there might be _other_ aspects of reality we cannot observe?

replace.claim: Is this not the best possible evidence that there might be _other_ aspects of reality we cannot observe?

inline.find: Though why limit ourselves to a “fourth” world? For all we know, there might be _infinitely_ many aspects of reality that we cannot observe today, or which may be fundamentally unobservable.

replace.claim: Though why limit ourselves to a “fourth” world? For all we know, there might be _infinitely_ many aspects of reality that we cannot observe today, or which may be fundamentally unobservable.

inline.find: But these tremendous unknowns as to the nature of reality should give us pause before rating consciousness as unique and morally important.

replace.claim: But these tremendous unknowns as to the nature of reality should give us pause before rating consciousness as unique and morally important.

inline.find: If we accept there could be _further_ aspects of reality that we cannot observe, not unlike consciousness, then we should not take for granted that consciousness is privileged among intelligent beings.

replace.claim: If we accept there could be _further_ aspects of reality that we cannot observe, not unlike consciousness, then we should not take for granted that consciousness is privileged among intelligent beings.

inline.find: given the existing evidence for alternative paths to cognition from terrestrial neurophysiology (e.g. machine intelligence), we ought to consider seriously whether there might be alternatives to phenomena _like_ consciousness, but substantively different.

replace.claim: given the existing evidence for alternative paths to cognition from terrestrial neurophysiology (e.g. machine intelligence), we ought to consider seriously whether there might be alternatives to phenomena _like_ consciousness, but substantively different.

inline.find: Physicalism is a popular and reasonable explanation of consciousness.

replace.claim: Physicalism is a popular and reasonable explanation of consciousness.

inline.find: A physicalist’s best guess should be that consciousness is somehow intrinsic to neurophysiology, otherwise we have to make strange ontological and scientific conclusions (like c-particles).

replace.claim: A physicalist’s best guess should be that consciousness is somehow intrinsic to neurophysiology, otherwise we have to make strange ontological and scientific conclusions (like c-particles).

inline.find: Once we assume consciousness is physically intrinsic to the neurophysiological process itself, it is no longer necessary to assume moral obligations to one aspect of that process.

replace.claim: Once we assume consciousness is physically intrinsic to the neurophysiological process itself, it is no longer necessary to assume moral obligations to one aspect of that process.

inline.find: We should instead assess moral obligations to the preferences exhibited by neurophysiology. Presumably, we also owe some obligations to any agent which exhibits preferences, but this is the responsibility of a moral framework to judge.

replace.claim: We should instead assess moral obligations to the preferences exhibited by neurophysiology. Presumably, we also owe some obligations to any agent which exhibits preferences, but this is the responsibility of a moral framework to judge.

inline.find: One can try to defend an obligation to consciousness by asserting it is special.

replace.claim: One can try to defend an obligation to consciousness by asserting it is special.

inline.find: However, if we cannot observe consciousness, we ought to suppose there could be further aspects of reality we cannot observe, similar or dissimilar to consciousness in ways we do not yet understand.

replace.claim: However, if we cannot observe consciousness, we ought to suppose there could be further aspects of reality we cannot observe, similar or dissimilar to consciousness in ways we do not yet understand.

inline.find: it would be premature to determine that consciousness demands unique obligations.

replace.claim: it would be premature to determine that consciousness demands unique obligations.

inline.find: Obviously, these final conclusions are several steps removed from ground truth.

replace.claim: Obviously, these final conclusions are several steps removed from ground truth.
